story:
People gather, and the buzz of anticipation feels almost physical. I sip champagne and scan the crowd. Someone catches my eye and then looks away; others do the same. The furtive glances make me uneasy, though Mum doesn’t notice. We’re on the Big Field for a Midsummer party, waiting for James. It’s nearly seven; he should be here.

Gentle, hypnotic music rides the warm breeze, threading through the fairy lights wrapped around the marquee. The tune is strange, one I’ve only heard since moving here. “Here” is Evergreen—“Twinned with Latrommi, Italy,” the sign says—a postcard-perfect English village: thatched roofs, roses round doors, Sunday cricket. When we arrived three months ago, we could barely find it. The GPS ignored it; we followed a paper map and scrawled directions down a winding lane so narrow it seemed to lead nowhere. Evergreen isn’t the kind of place you stumble upon; you have to be looking for it.

People are friendly, though a little closed. It’s only for twelve months, I tell myself; my secondment will end. Still, the party feels odd and I can’t say why. The music grows louder; the last stragglers cross the lawn in the long gold light of evening. I scan for James, wanting moral support. As I wait, my thoughts snag on something I’ve ignored: everyone here seems very well. No sniffles, no sore backs, no hay fever. I can’t remember seeing a doctor’s surgery or dentist at all—no medical anything.

A kiss lands on my temple.

“There you are,” James says, and kisses Mum’s cheek. We present our invites at the door, oddly formal for a village do. The invitation—In Honour of Latrommi—arrived days ago, including Mum, which I’d found kind. She’s only visiting. Since Dad died, she’s been lonely. Her eighty-fifth falls on Saturday; I’d planned a quiet dinner in the next village. But when the invite came—for the same night—Mum smiled. “A party would do me good,” she said. “We can celebrate all the same.”

Inside, it’s breathtaking—fresh flowers, fairy lights, gold organza. “How are you feeling?” I ask.

“Fabulous, darling,” she trills, eyes bright. Champagne suits her. A few older ladies beam and whisk her away, laughing. She waves before disappearing toward the buffet.

“Unusual, isn’t it?” I say to James. We’ve seen parties on the Big Field before but never been invited.

He grins. “Maybe we’ve proved our worth.” He dodges the champagne and heads to the bar.

As I find seats, the oddness sharpens. The elderly here are sprightly. No walking sticks. Hardly any glasses. James returns, frowning.

“Shortcut through the cemetery,” he says. “I know the yew trees made it gloomy even this early, but the headstones looked… off. I’m pretty sure everyone died at eighty-five. Every single one.”

Before I can answer, a commotion at the front.

“Happy Birthday to you!” Everyone sings and claps. Mum is onstage, twirling, the centre of attention. An elderly woman drops into the chair opposite us, eyes glassy with drink and something like grief.

“My turn next month,” she slurs. “Eighty-five. There’s always a party. The last party. We aren’t allowed to live forever, you know. It’s against the rules.”

“This isn’t Mum’s party,” I start, but she shakes her head.

“Oh yes, it is. She just doesn’t know it.”

Cold dread snaps into place.

“Mum!”

I run, heels skidding on the grass. Hands grab at me from the crowd, slowing me, as James barrels after me. I reach the stage in time to see Mum hustled through the back curtain.

I freeze. The strange looks. The youthful faces. The graveyard. The parties at eighty-five. Evergreen twinned with Latrommi—Latrommi, immortal spelled backward. We aren’t allowed to live forever. Not here. Not past eighty-five.

And tonight is Mum’s turn.
